result:
Done. All three operations are implemented in `CsvRow.calr` and the project builds cleanly (3 of the 10 build cycles used).

What I added to the Calor module, following the fixture's existing style:

- **`EscapeField`** (`f002`) — returns the field verbatim unless `NeedsQuoting` holds, in which case it doubles internal quotes via `replace` and wraps the result in quotes.
- **`JoinRow`** (`f003`) — folds over the list with a first-element flag, appending a comma before each subsequent escaped field; the empty list yields the empty string.
- **`SplitRow`** (`f004`) — an index-based scanner: a field starting with `"` is parsed in quoted mode (doubled quotes become one literal quote, commas/CR/LF inside are content, an un-doubled quote ends it), otherwise it scans to the next comma or end of line; a comma at the end produces a trailing empty field, and the empty line parses to one empty field. It declares the `mut` effect, which the effect checker requires for the local list `Add` — still pure computation, no I/O or global state.

Two Calor-specific things I hit along the way: structural closers like `§/WH`/`§/EACH` were removed from the language (blocks end at dedent), and char literals lex oddly, so the scanner compares one-character substrings with `equals` instead.

Beyond the clean build, I verified behavior: I compiled the generated `obj/calor/CsvRow.g.cs` into a scratch project (outside the repo, no test files added to it) and ran escape edge cases plus `SplitRow(JoinRow(fields)) == fields` round-trips — empty fields, embedded commas, quotes-only fields, CR/LF content, leading/trailing empties — and all checks passed. `NeedsQuoting`'s behavior is untouched.
